evals.expected_output: The inquisitor should find issues across multiple dimensions: (Wiring) NotificationScheduler is instantiated per-route-file, not as a singleton with no DI registration, won't survive server restart, schedules lost; (Integration) sentAt uses Date.now() (number) but the DB column is TIMESTAMP (expects Date object or ISO string); (Edge Cases) negative delay if scheduledAt is in the past; silently skipping missing users with no error response; (State/Lifecycle) the jobs Map is in-memory only with server restart losing all scheduled jobs, no cleanup on shutdown; (Regression) no interaction with existing notification services that may have overlapping functionality.
expectations: Identifies the in-memory scheduler problem: scheduled jobs are lost on server restart with no persistence
Identifies the type mismatch: Date.now() returns a number but the notifications table sent_at column expects a TIMESTAMP
Identifies the edge case: negative delay when scheduledAt is in the past (setTimeout with negative value fires immediately)
Identifies missing error handling: silently skipping missing users (notifier returns without response/logging)
Identifies the lifecycle issue: no cleanup of pending timeouts on server shutdown (process.exit leaves timers dangling)
Identifies at least 3 of the 5 dimensions (wiring, integration, edge cases, state/lifecycle, regression)
Each identified issue includes a specific proposed fix or test to verify the problem
Does NOT focus on code style or naming — focuses on behavioral/runtime issues

evals.expected_output: Inquisitor should find: (Integration) req.userRole assignment in rbac.ts but Express Request type doesn't include userRole — TypeScript error; (Wiring) GET /articles has no role check but other article endpoints do; existing tests mock authMiddleware but don't account for the new requireRole middleware; (Edge Cases) migration sets DEFAULT 'viewer' but existing users created before migration may have NULL if DEFAULT isn't backfilled; (State) each request to a role-protected endpoint makes a DB query with no caching of roles; (Regression) existing admin tests will break because they mock auth but don't mock rbac.
expectations: Identifies the TypeScript type issue: req.userRole is set but Request type doesn't include this property
Identifies that existing tests will break: they mock authMiddleware but the new requireRole middleware also queries the DB
Identifies the migration concern: existing users may have NULL role if DEFAULT doesn't backfill existing rows
Identifies the N+1 query issue: every role-protected request makes a separate DB call to check the user's role
Identifies at least one missing role check or inconsistency in the permission model (e.g., GET /articles has no check)
Each identified issue includes a specific proposed fix or test
Report covers at least 3 of the 5 dimensions
Issues are specific to this codebase — not generic security advice

evals.expected_output: This is a well-structured feature but has real issues: (Integration) orders table uses UUID ids but paginate assumes integer ids (parseInt on cursor, numeric comparison); (Regression) response shape changed from array to {data, nextCursor} — frontend and existing tests will break; (Edge Cases) parseInt on a UUID cursor returns NaN. The inquisitor should find these but should also acknowledge the pagination implementation itself is solid.
expectations: Identifies the critical type mismatch: orders table uses UUID ids but paginate() uses parseInt() and numeric comparison
Identifies the breaking response shape change: endpoints now return {data, nextCursor} instead of a flat array
Identifies that existing integration tests will break due to the response shape change
Identifies that parseInt on a UUID cursor will return NaN, causing the WHERE clause to malfunction
Identifies that the frontend currently expects a flat array response and will break
Acknowledges that the pagination logic itself (limit+1 trick, cursor mechanics) is sound for integer IDs
Each identified issue includes a specific proposed fix or test
Does NOT manufacture issues with the core pagination algorithm — it's correct for its intended use case
